title: 6 Tir — The Circumcision of Christ & The Assembly
narrative:
The sixth day of Tir commemorates the Circumcision of the Lord Jesus Christ, which was performed on the eighth day after His birth in accordance with the commandment given to Abraham and renewed through Moses — the commandment that every male child of the covenant must bear in his flesh the sign of the covenant between God and His people. The feast of the Circumcision is a mystery of sublime paradox: the Lawgiver submits to the Law; the one who instituted the covenant of circumcision as a foreshadowing of a greater covenant accepts in His own flesh the cut that points beyond itself toward the baptism He will institute. The fathers, from Origen onward, have read the Circumcision as a type of baptism — the old covenant's sign of entry into the people of God prefigures the new covenant's sign of entry through water and the Spirit — and in the eighth day of the Lord's life they have seen the eschatological eighth day, the day beyond the seven days of creation, the day of the new creation that baptism inaugurates.

The holy Prophet Ezekiel, whose monthly commemoration falls on this sixth day of Tir, is peculiarly suited to the feast of the Circumcision by virtue of his oracle in the thirty-sixth chapter of his prophecy: 'I will sprinkle clean water upon you, and you shall be clean from all your uncleannesses; and I will give you a new heart, and a new spirit I will put within you.' This prophecy of the new covenant, of the washing that purifies the heart and the Spirit that animates the new creation, is among the most direct Old Testament anticipations of the baptismal theology of the New Testament; and Ezekiel, who prophesied it, stands in this month of the Epiphany as the great prophetic voice behind the hallowing of the waters. The circumcision of the flesh gives way, in Ezekiel's vision, to the circumcision of the heart — and that circumcision of the heart is performed by the water and the Spirit.

Saint Theophanes the Recluse, the nineteenth-century Russian bishop and monastic theologian whose name means 'manifestation of God' — again the Tir word, theophania — is commemorated on this day. Theophanes resigned his episcopal office to enter a hermitage where he spent the remaining decades of his life in writing, prayer, and counsel by correspondence, producing a body of ascetical and spiritual theology that has nourished the Russian Church for a century and a half. His writings on the interior life — particularly his translation and adaptation of the Philokalia and his own Unseen Warfare — describe the inner circumcision of the heart that the baptismal grace is meant to accomplish: the cutting away of the passions, the submission of the will to the divine will, the progressive transformation of the interior man into the image of God.

The theological seal of this sixth day of Tir is the mystery of voluntary submission to the covenant. The Lord who has no obligation to the Law submits to the Law's cutting sign, not because He needs it but because the submission itself is the inauguration of the new obedience that will culminate at the Jordan and on the cross. Ezekiel prophesies the interior counterpart of this outward sign; Theophanes teaches its daily practice in the life of the baptized. The Circumcision of the Lord is thus the first of the great epiphanic acts of His public life: the first time that the body of the incarnate Word bears, by His own will and for our sake, the mark of the covenant people to whom He has come.
